coredata.dc:title: Significantly activated persulfate by novel carbon quantum dots-modified N-BiOCl for complete degradation of bisphenol-A under visible light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jianqiao
Li, Zhiyang
Lei, Qian
Zhong, Dan
Ke, YiXin
Liu, WenJie
Yang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: The practical application of bismuth-based photocatalysts in the field of micropollutant photodegradation is limited due to their weak light absorption and rapid charge recombination. Herein, we have developed a novel carbon quantum dots-modified N-BiOCl (CDs-N-BiOCl) photocatalyst to activate persulfate (PS) for the complete elimination of endocrine-disruptor bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light irradiation. The photoelectric properties characterization shows that N atoms could replace Cl atoms or adsorb on Bi atoms to form local N 1s states in the BiOCl lattice, accompanied by the introduction of doping energy levels that shorten the electron migration distance. Meanwhile, the decorated CDs could effectively accept the photoinduced electrons from N-BiOCl conduction band to facilitate the charge separation. Thus, the 7%CDs-N-BiOCl (7CNB) nanocomposite synergistically activated PS realized rapid and effective degradation of BPA within 20 min (degradation efficiency and mineralization reached 100 % and 66.4 % respectively). Moreover, the 7CNB/PS system displayed favorable adaptability, durability, and interference resistance. Furthermore, the biotoxicity experiments demonstrated that the photodegradation intermediates promoted the growth of Escherichia coli which indicates its eco-friendliness for practical application. Finally, the electron transfer mechanism and the formation of reactive oxygen species in the photodegradation process were interpreted. In short, this work will present a promising strategy for bismuth-based photocatalysts to be used for the efficient treatment of real water bodies under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Significantly activated persulfate by novel carbon quantum dots-modified N-BiOCl for complete degradation of bisphenol-A under visible light irradiation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The practical application of bismuth-based photocatalysts in the field of micropollutant photodegradation is limited due to their weak light absorption and rapid charge recombination. Herein, we have developed a novel carbon quantum dots-modified N-BiOCl (CDs-N-BiOCl) photocatalyst to activate persulfate (PS) for the complete elimination of endocrine-disruptor bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light irradiation. The photoelectric properties characterization shows that N atoms could replace Cl atoms or adsorb on Bi atoms to form local N 1s states in the BiOCl lattice, accompanied by the introduction of doping energy levels that shorten the electron migration distance. Meanwhile, the decorated CDs could effectively accept the photoinduced electrons from N-BiOCl conduction band to facilitate the charge separation. Thus, the 7%CDs-N-BiOCl (7CNB) nanocomposite synergistically activated PS realized rapid and effective degradation of BPA within 20 min (degradation efficiency and mineralization reached 100 % and 66.4 % respectively). Moreover, the 7CNB/PS system displayed favorable adaptability, durability, and interference resistance. Furthermore, the biotoxicity experiments demonstrated that the photodegradation intermediates promoted the growth of Escherichia coli which indicates its eco-friendliness for practical application. Finally, the electron transfer mechanism and the formation of reactive oxygen species in the photodegradation process were interpreted. In short, this work will present a promising strategy for bismuth-based photocatalysts to be used for the efficient treatment of real water bodies under visible light irradiation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive use of endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs) has led to an increase in aquatic pollution which poses a potential hazard to biodiversity and human health (Ghassabian et al., 2022). As a typical EDCs, bisphenol A (BPA) can accumulate in living organisms, especially in human beings through the food chain, which affects the normal endocrine function of the organism (Charitos et al., 2022). Therefore, there is an urgent need to establish effective and economical treatment technologies for the removal of BPA from actual water bodies.

Compared with conventional remediation solutions, which are cumbersome and require additional maintenance (Moradi et al., 2021a; Zhang et al., 2020), sulfate radical (SO4 −) based photocatalysis by activated persulfate (PS) or peroxymonosulfate (PMS) is proven as an environmentally friendly technology for the removal of EDCs due to its strong oxidative and eco-friendly properties (Zhao et al., 2021). Moreover, SO4 −-based photocatalysis has higher redox potential, longer half-life, and adaptability in wide pH conditions than hydroxyl radicals (OH) (Yang et al., 2019). Currently, various metal oxides have been developed as photocatalysts (Wang et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021), although bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl) has shown superior degradation performance in the field of pollutant removal due to its unique [Bi2O2]2+-[Cl2]2− layered structure and stable physicochemical properties (Zhao et al., 2022b). However, Huang et al. synthesized BiOCl nanoplates by hydrothermal method, which could activate PMS to degrade BPA with a removal rate of only 31.1 % (Huang et al., 2021). This indicates that BiOCl has some limitations such as weak visible light absorption and faster carrier recombination, resulting in limited photocatalytic activity under visible light illumination (Zhao et al., 2022b). Therefore, suitable modification strategies are required to enhance the visible light absorption of BiOCl to take full advantage of solar energy.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been considered a promising material for BiOCl modification due to their outstanding photonic conversion and electron transfer characteristics (Zhou et al., 2021). The presence of CDs not only enhances the built-in electric field of BiOCl to enhance the interfacial charge transfer and reduce the in situ recombination of carriers during migration but also strengthens the optical absorption of the photocatalyst and provides enough reactive sites for the photocatalytic reaction (Zhu et al., 2022). However, the photoluminescence emission range (430–550 nm) of CDs and the optical absorption (∼350 nm) of pristine BiOCl do not match each other (Xu et al., 2019), resulting in insufficient enhancement of the photoelectron transfer efficiency of CDs in the reported CDs-BiOCl photocatalysts. Therefore, the energy band structure of the BiOCl semiconductor should be tuned to match the photoluminescence of CDs.

In general, heteroatom doping is a facile and effective solution to modify the intrinsic band gap of photocatalysts to expand visible light absorption. The existence of heteroatoms could form new valence band (VB) energy levels or electron donor energy levels between BiOCl and other elements, which could be beneficial for band gap reduction. Moreover, heteroatom doping could effectively induce charge delocalization to improve electron transfer (Liang et al., 2020). Guo et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped BiOCl (N-BiOCl) by hydrothermal method (Guo et al., 2022). The N atom in the lattice structure of BiOCl could substitute the Cl atom or attach to the Bi atom to form an intermediate energy level and shorten the electron transfer distance. Notably, the studied N-BiOCl photocatalysts are still limited by the fast in situ recombination of charges (Chen et al., 2015). In conclusion, it is necessary to evaluate the mutual effects of co-modifying BiOCl with CDs loading and N-doping on photocatalytic performance.

Inspired by the above, N-BiOCl was prepared by ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal method to construct a novel CDs-N-BiOCl (CNB) photocatalyst by loading CDs via a one-step calcination process, which synergistically activated PS for enhanced degradation of micro-pollutants. The effects of CDs loading and N doping on the properties of BiOCl morphology, crystalline phase, chemical state, and optical absorption were systematically investigated by various characterization techniques. Meanwhile, the effects of different reaction conditions on the degradation efficiency of pollutants were investigated on the basis of optimized doping ratios in conjunction with practical applications. In addition, the ecotoxicity of BPA and its photodegradation intermediates was assessed by the survival density of Gram bacteria. The BPA degradation mechanism and pathway were presented on the basis of electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and radical quenching experiments. In short, this work demonstrates that tuning the energy band structure and visible light absorption of metal oxides through facile strategies could enhance contaminant degradation, which is significant for the use of solar energy for drinking water safety and maintaining the health of the natural water environment.

Bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), urea (CH4N2O), citric acid (C6H8O7), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), nitric acid (HNO3), ethanol (C2H5OH), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were used for the preparation of photocatalyst. Bisphenol A (BPA), potassium persulfate (PS), rhodamine B (RhB), acetaminophen (AAP), phenol, sodium chloride (NaCl), humic acid (HA), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) were purchased from Macklin (China) for the photocatalytic performance evaluation. Methanol (MeOH), tert-butanol (TBA), p-benzoquinone (BQ), and EDTA-2Na were purchased from Aladdin for reactive oxygen species (ROS) quenching experiments. The spin-trapping agents 5,5-dimethyl-1-pryrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) were used to trap ROS. Tryptone, yeast powder, agar, and sodium chloride were purchased for culturing E. coli and B. subtilis. Chromatographic-grade acetonitrile and methanol (Fisher Scientific, USA) were used for ultra-high performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) analysis. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was used throughout the experiment.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method (Zhang et al., 2018). Briefly, 3.0 g of citric acid and 1.0 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. The obtained solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged to extract the supernatant and vacuum freeze-dried to obtain CDs.

The white N-doped BiOCl photocatalysts were synthesized by an ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal method. Specifically, 2 mmoL of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and KCl were dissolved in 20 mL of 1 mol/L HNO3 and 30 mL of ultrapure water, respectively. The two solutions were mixed with stirring to develop a white suspension. The suspension pH was tuned to 7 by adding ethylenediamine dropwise. Subsequently, the solution was stirred continuously for 30 min and then placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave for 24 h at 180 °C. Finally, the product was washed several times with ultrapure water. The precipitate was collected and dried to obtain white N-BiOCl, referred to as NB. BiOCl samples were prepared by adjusting the solution pH to 7 using 1 mM NaOH.

CDs-N-BiOCl nanocomposites were constructed by calcination method. In a typical synthesis process, 35 mg of CDs was dissolved in anhydrous ethanol following the addition of 465 mg of N-doped BiOCl precursor and the mixture was heated at 40 °C and stirred until all the anhydrous ethanol evaporated. The powder was calcined at 300 °C for 3 h with a ramping rate of 5 °C/min to obtain 7%CDs-N-BiOCl, named as 7CNB. 7%CDs-BiOCl was prepared in the same manner and named 7CB. Similarly, CDs-N-BiOCl with different ratios were prepared by varying the mass of CDs and named 1CNB, 3CNB, 5CNB, 10CNB, and 15CNB.

The morphological characteristics of the synthesized photocatalysts were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). The crystal properties, functional group structures, and thermal stability of BOC, NB, 7CB, and 7CNB were investigated by X-ray diffractometer (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), and thermogravimetric (TGA). The elemental composition and bonding information of the as-prepared samples were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The Optical and electrochemical properties were investigated through UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS), photoluminescence (PL) spectrum, and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). Furthermore, the mineralization of BPA after photodegradation was evaluated using total organic carbon (TOC). Reaction intermediates were analyzed by high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC/MS). The instrument types and models involved are listed in Text S1.

The photocatalytic performance of the reaction system was assessed by the BPA photodegradation by CNB-activated PS under visible light irradiation. Moreover, the influence of different reaction conditions on the removal efficiency was investigated. Furthermore, the potential ROS in the system was detected by ROS quenching experiments and EPR at room temperature. The experimental details are described in Text S2. Finally, the biotoxicity of the reaction filtrate was assessed by the growth trends of two representative Gram bacteria (E. coli and B. subtilis), whose culture procedures are shown in Text S3.

The pristine BOC, NB, and 7CNB, present a huge number of stacked ultrathin nanosheets with ordered and smooth shapes while few of them are assembled into a three-dimensional flower-like structure (Fig. 1a–c). Generally, this layered structure of nanosheets generates multiple reflections which are beneficial for the enhancement of light-harvesting efficiency (Wu et al., 2016). Moreover, the specific surface areas of BOC, NB, and 7CNB were 11.46 m2/g, 10.84 m2/g, and 9.49 m2/g, respectively (Fig. S1a–c). These findings indicate that the doping of N and CDs has no significant effect on the specific surface area and nanosheet morphology of BOC. The prepared carbon quantum dots showed a spherical shape with an average particle size of about 12.87 nm (Fig. S2). It can be seen that the BOC, NB, and 7CNB have nanosheet structures (Fig. 1d–f), which is consistent with SEM. Due to the low electronic contrast, the fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern was used to better distinguish the specific location and morphological features of CDs. All samples could be observed with diffraction ring corresponding to the BiOCl (110) crystal plane (insets of Fig. 1d–f). In addition, the FFT pattern of 7CNB appeared as a distinct diffraction ring with a lattice spacing of 0.207 nm compared to that of BOC and NB, which may be related to the (102) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (Li et al., 2018b), suggesting that CDs have been successfully attached to the BOC surface. The above results indicate that 7CNB could maintain the same exposed crystal plane with BOC, and NB after the one-step calcination method treatment.

As shown in Fig. 2a, pristine BOC displays characteristic diffraction peaks at 5.54°, 11.05°, 11.87°, 14.89°, 15.26°, 15.89°, 16.61°, 18.61°, 21.11°, 21.82°, 22.37°, 24.28°, 26.14°, 26.96°, 30.02°, 32.63°, 33.64°, which are assigned to (001), (002), (101), (110), (102), (111), (003), (112), (200), (201), (113), (211), (212), (114), (220), (214), (310) crystal planes of tetragonal BOC (JCPDS 06-0249) (Qi et al., 2022). For the NB, 7CB, and 7CNB, the successful doping of N and CDs could be confirmed by the magnified XRD images at 2θ = 11.5°–12.5° (Fig. 2b). Based on the atomic radius order of Cl > C > N > O, it can easily happen that Cl or O atoms are partially replaced by N and C atoms, which leads to the shift of diffraction peaks for 7CNB. Apparently, the diffraction peak of the other three samples is shifted in a large angular direction compared with that of the pristine BOC, indicating that some N or C atoms could have occupied the BOC lattice and thus replaced the Cl atoms instead of the O atoms (Guo et al., 2022). As for the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2c), the signals at 3400 and 1630 cm−1 for BOC, NB, and 7CNB are attributed to the typical stretching and bending vibrations of OH caused by water adsorption on the surface, while the peak at 540 cm−1 belongs to the BiO bond (Luo et al., 2018). Moreover, the TGA curves show that all prepared photocatalysts have the main weight loss interval occurring at 650–750 °C (Fig. 2d), which may be due to the BOC decomposition, indicating the favorable thermal stability of 7CNB for the photocatalytic reaction at room temperature.

To demonstrate the presence of N and CDs in the 7CNB photocatalyst, the XPS using the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV as calibration was employed. XPS spectrum shows that the BOC series photocatalysts are mainly composed of C, Bi, O, and Cl, where the C element may come from the adventitious carbon from the instrument (Fig. 3a). In the C 1s spectrum, pristine BOC at 284.8 eV was fitted as a dominant peak which belongs to the CC/CC bond as displayed in Fig. 3b. In contrast, the 7CNB presents three peaks at 284.8 (CC/CC), 286.01 (CO) and 288.37 eV (CO), respectively (Zhao et al., 2022a). The occurrence of COBi and CO proves the successful doping of CDs and the chemical bonding between CDs and NB. As shown in Fig. 3c, no distinctive characteristic peaks were observed in the N 1s spectrum of the pristine BOC. However, the N 1s XPS peak of 7CNB can be divided into two peaks at 399.14 eV (OBiN) and 400.52 eV (BiON) (Guo et al., 2022). Moreover, in Fig. S3, the N 1s spectrum of CDs was divided into two peaks at 388.89 eV and 401.4 eV, which belong to CNC and N(C)3 (Zhang et al., 2022). For NB, the peaks attributed to OBiN and BiON were also retained at 399.24 eV and 401.1 eV respectively, indicating that N has been doped into the BOC lattice before loading the CDs.

In Fig. 3d, BOC (7CNB) has two peaks at 159.52 eV (159.35 eV) and 164.82 eV (164.65 eV) corresponding to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+, respectively (Liang et al., 2020). Apparently, the peak of 7CNB is shifted by 0.17 eV toward the lower binding energy, which may be caused by the attraction of Bi3+ to the surrounding electrons after the doping of N and CDs. It can be seen in Fig. 3e that the O 1s peaks of BOC can be divided into three peaks with central binding energies at 530.28, 531.46, and 532.99 eV, which are designated as BiO, hydroxyl groups, and CO, respectively (Fu et al., 2020; Kim and Jo, 2019). The two peaks exhibited by 7CNB include 530.01 and 531.39 eV are attributed to BiO and OH. Compared with BOC, the shift of the BiO bond to a lower binding energy in 7CNB is probably due to the substitution of N or C for the Cl atom resulting in a higher electron density. In Fig. 3f, the two dominant signals of Cl are noticed at 198.22 eV (Cl 2p3/2) and 199.82 eV (Cl 2p1/2) for BOC (Liu et al., 2019). In contrast, the Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 of 7CNB are shifted by 0.11 eV toward the lower binding energy which may be attributed to the injection of free electrons from CDs into the BOC conduction band (CB). It can be seen that the doping of N and CDs tunes the built-in electric field of BiOCl which also indicates the successful doping of N and CDs in 7CNB is successful.

The optical properties of the prepared samples were studied by UV–vis DRS. In Fig. 4a, the optical absorption cut-off edge of the pristine BOC was located at 353 nm while the edge extended to 358 nm and 405 nm for NB and 7CB which might be due to the replacement of the Cl atom in the BOC lattice by N or C elements. As expected, when the same mass fraction of CDs with effective visible light absorption was loaded onto NB, the absorption edge of 7CNB was extended from 405 nm to 455 nm and the intensity of the absorption tail peak was further enhanced compared to that of 7CB indicating that the introduction of N-doped energy level fully exploited the photonic conversion property of CDs. Moreover, the calculated band gap energies (Eg) of BOC, NB, 7CB, and 7CNB were 3.34 eV, 3.32 eV, 2.78 eV, and 2.37 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b) (Qi et al., 2022). The above results show that the CDs loading and N doping are favorable for the red shift of the optical absorption edge and the overall enhancement of visible light absorption.

The EIS Nyquist plots of nanomaterials are shown in Fig. 4c. Generally, the semi-arc radius is inversely proportional to the charge transfer efficiency (Xia et al., 2020). 7CNB shows the smallest Nyquist curve radius which indicates that the presence of CDs and N lowers the electron migration potential barrier to promote the interfacial charge transfer in the photocatalyst. Subsequently, the synthesized pristine BOC was identified as an n-type semiconductor due to the positive slope of the Mott-Schottky curve (Fig. 4d). The flat-band potentials of BOC and 7CNB were estimated to be 0.16 eV and 0.18 eV vs. SCE, respectively. This was converted to the value relative to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) by using the following equation (Eq. (1)):
(1)

The flat-band potentials of BOC and 7CNB were equivalent to 0.4 eV and 0.42 eV vs. NHE. Universally, the flat-band potential could be considered as the Fermi energy level (EF) of the semiconductor (Tian et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the band edge potential of photocatalysts was calculated by the following equation (Eq. (2)):
(2)
E CB
=
E VB

E g

Based on the VB-XPS results (Fig. 4e), the VB edges of BOC and 7CNB were 2.11 and 1.76 eV lower than the position of EF, respectively. Therefore, the VB of BOC and 7CNB was 2.51 eV and 2.18 eV vs. NHE, respectively. The CB of BOC and 7CNB were supposed to be −0.83 eV and −0.19 eV vs. NHE, respectively (calculated through band edge potential equation while the value of Eg was obtained from Fig. 4b). Fig. 4f depicts the schematic energy levels of 7CNB and BOC (Table S1), which clearly shows the band gap reduction due to N doping and CDs loading that significantly enhances the visible light absorption and trapping.

Apart from narrowing the band gap, effective migration and separation of photogenerated carriers are equally essential for the enhancement of photocatalytic performance. The charge recombination of 7CNB in the photodegradation of pollutants was investigated by PL spectroscopy. Lower PL intensity corresponds to a lower electron-hole (e
−
-h
+ ) recombination rate (Zhong et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 4g, the pristine BOC shows the highest PL emission peak at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, representing the rapid in situ recombination of photogenerated electrons. The introduction of N significantly reduced the PL intensity of NB indicating the presence of N-doped energy levels could effectively trap part of the photoinduced electrons. Apparently, after further modification by CDs, 7CNB displays the lowest PL intensity, implying that charge recombination is significantly inhibited. Interestingly, the carrier separation efficiency would be weakened with the increase of the CDs ratio, which could be due to the excess CDs inducing the e
−
-h
+ pairs to undergo recombination (Li et al., 2018b). Additionally, the charge separation of the photocatalysts was analyzed by time-resolved PL spectroscopy (Fig. 4h–i). The decay time constants (τA) of BOC, NB, and 7CNB were calculated using a multi-exponential fit as listed in Table S2. The τA of BOC and NB were 0.9262 ns and 0.9686 ns, suggesting that the N doping shortens the electron migration distance. Conversely, the τA of 7CNB was calculated to be 0.8406 ns, which is probably due to the photoinduced electrons located at the NB CB could be rapidly transferred to the surface of CDs, thus effectively delaying the in-situ recombination of charges. These results demonstrate that 7CNB photocatalyst with full visible light absorption and effective charge separation was successfully constructed.

To assess the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized photocatalysts, visible-light driven degradation experiments were performed on BPA (used as a representative pollutant). As shown in Fig. 5a, the photolysis (visible light alone) and dark reaction showed almost negligible degradation of BPA even with the addition of PS about 31.88 % and 48.39 % of BPA were removed by the BOC/PS and NB/PS systems after 20 min, which may be due to the wide band gap and ineffective charge separation for BOC and NB. Subsequently, 90.65 % of BPA was photo-decomposed by 7CNB nanomaterials in the absence of PS due to its unique features such as efficient visible light absorption and charge separation. As envisioned, the 7CNB/PS system achieved 100 % BPA removal with the synergistic effect of PS. It is noteworthy that the BPA photodegradation efficiencies of the 1CNB/PS, 3CNB/PS, 5CNB/PS, 7CNB/PS, 10CNB/PS and 15CNB/PS systems were 52.62 %, 63.04 %, 87.32 %, 100 %, 76.69 % and 74.12 %, respectively (Fig. 5b). When the doping amount of CDs was too low, its unique photon conversion and charge separation properties could not be fully utilized. As the doping amount was further increased beyond 7 %, excessive CDs would act as a complex center for photoinduced e
−
-h
+ pairs to inhibit the reaction proceeding. Moreover, according to the pseudo-first-order rate equation ln
C
C 0
= − kt (Kalantary et al., 2018; Osouleddini et al., 2019), the apparent rate constant for BPA removal by 7CNB/PS was calculated to be 0.3646 min−1, which was 21.32, 2.99, and 1.5 times higher than that of BOC/PS, 7CNB, and 7CB/PS, respectively (Fig. 5c). The highest TOC removal (66.4 %) was noticed in 7CNB/PS system for BPA as shown in Fig. 5d. The enhanced photocatalytic activity is attributed to the N doping and CDs loading that broaden the optical absorption and tune the energy band structure of BOC. That is, N atoms could replace the Cl atoms in the BiOCl lattice or adsorb on the Bi atoms to form local N 1s states accompanying the introduction of midgap states to shorten the electron hopping distance. Meanwhile, the CDs loading could effectively accept the photogenerated electrons from NB CB to promote charge separation efficiency.

To evaluate the potential of 7CNB/PS system for practical applications, the influence of prevalent variables on the reaction rate of pollutants was studied. The 7CNB/PS system could rapidly degrade BPA within 5 min when the initial concentration of BPA was 5 mg/L (10 mg/L) as presented in Fig. 6a. With the increase in pollutant concentration, the degradation efficiency was suppressed which might be due to the limited amount of ROS produced by the 7CNB and PS per unit time. Moreover, high concentrations of pollutant molecules could not only occupy the active site of the photocatalyst but also absorb photons, weakening the direct contact between the photocatalyst and the photons thus leading to reduced photocatalytic activity (Moradi et al., 2021b). Also, as the concentration of pollutants increases, more intermediates could be produced that compete with BPA for ROS. Accordingly, the role between 7CNB and PS on the photodegradation efficiency of BPA needs to be further investigated. As shown in Fig. 6b, with the addition of 1 mM PS, the degradation rate of BPA increased significantly (from 90.65 % to 100 %). However, when the PS concentration exceeded 1 mM, the limited degree of enhancement of the removal efficiency was probably attributed to the inhibition of the ROS generation by the self-quenching reaction of PS or the lack of free electrons and active sites on the catalyst surface that limited the activation of excess PS (Vasseghian et al., 2020). Correspondingly, the degradation efficiency of BPA was gradually increased to 100 % when the dose of 7CNB was increased from 0.1 g/L to 1.0 g/L (Fig. 6c). However, an excessive photocatalyst dose (1.5 g/L) would probably produce a loss of total surface area due to nanomaterial agglomeration, accompanied by self-quenching of free radicals and photo-shielding effect, which prevent further enhancement in degradation efficiency (Xie et al., 2019). In addition, the excess active sites could be occupied by PS thus weakening the access between PS and micro-contaminants with activated sites (Moradi et al., 2020). Therefore, the suggested optimal dosage for economic reasons and time cost is 10 mg/L BPA, 1 mM PS, 1.0 g/L 7CNB for the 7CNB/PS system.

The complex and variable pH value in the actual wastewater is an influential factor affecting the photodegradation performance. The 7CNB/PS system could completely remove BPA after 20 min of visible-light irradiation when the reaction solution was acidic, neutral, or even weakly alkaline conditions as shown in Fig. 6d. However, the BPA removal efficiency decreased to 77.45 % at pH = 11. In fact, this phenomenon was investigated by measuring the Zeta potential of 7CNB at different pH conditions (Fig. S4). The surface of 7CNB was negatively charged over a wide pH range, while the pKa of BPA and PS were identified as 9.6 and 9.4, respectively (Gao et al., 2010; Xu et al., 2018). When the solution pH was below 9.4, the Coulomb attraction between BPA or PS and the 7CNB surface favors contact with each other. Especially the photogenerated positive holes (h+) at acidic pH could oxidize BPA more efficiently (Amiri et al., 2022; Kalantary et al., 2019). As the pH exceeds 9.6, the dissociation of BPA and PS leads to the existence of Coulomb repulsion which is not conducive to the subsequent reaction. In particular, the high concentration of OH− would react with SO4 − to impede the redox process (Wei et al., 2022). In addition, the carbon on the surface of 7CNB under alkaline conditions could be functionalized by hydroxyl ions, which inhibited the adsorption of PS ions via Coulomb repulsion (Amiri et al., 2022; Moradi et al., 2017). Providentially, the final BPA degradation efficiency of this reaction system over a wide pH range is still appreciable due to the CDs loading and N doping as the BOC built-in electric field driver, indicating that the established visible-light-driven 7CNB/PS system is not highly dependent on the solution pH and has promising potential for practical applications.

Generally, photocatalyst recyclability is a critical aspect in assessing its practical application. As shown in Fig. 6e, the 7CNB/PS system was still able to completely remove BPA for 5 consecutive cycles after 20 min visible-light illumination, indicating the superior photocatalytic stability of the system. Meanwhile, RhB, AAP, and phenol were used as representatives of dyes, drugs, and phenols to evaluate the applicability of the photocatalysts (Fig. 6f). The removal efficiencies of the 7CNB/PS system for BPA, RhB, AAP, and phenol were 100 %, 97.47 %, 92.36 %, and 30.06 %, respectively. The significant difference in the degradation efficiency between phenol and other micropollutants could be attributed to the fact that phenol has the delocalized π7 8 bond, while the sp3 hybrid orbitals (CH bond) of RhB, BPA, and AAP enhance their potential for oxidation by ROS (Yang et al., 2019). In summary, the 7CNB/PS system has favorable stability and non-selective oxidation for the degradation of various pollutants.

The effect of coexisting anions (Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2− and NO3 −) and natural organic matter (NOM) on the photodegradation efficiency of BPA were investigated (Fig. 6g). At the same concentration (10 mM) of addition, Cl−, SO4 2−, and CO3 2− all inhibited the photocatalytic performance of the 7CNB/PS system, except for NO3 −. Specifically, NO3 − could react with water to generate ·OH to rapidly oxidize BPA within 10 min (Eq. (3)) (Si et al., 2018). Moreover, the CB position (−0.19 eV vs. NHE) of 7CNB is sufficiently negative to allow the reduction of NO3 − to NO2 − (E0 NO3-/NO2- = 0.94 eV, vs. NHE), N2 (E0 NO3-/N2 = 1.25 eV, vs. NHE) or NH4 + (E0 NO3-/NH4+ = 1.20 eV, vs. NHE) by electrons, which potentially inhibits the in situ recombination of e
−
-h
+ pairs promoting the formation of h+ thereby enhancing the removal efficiency of BPA (Cao et al., 2022). On the contrary, Cl− could react with ROS and especially quench h+ to from ·Cl, thus affecting the degradation process (Cao et al., 2023; Duan et al., 2016). Moreover, the hydrolysis of CO3 2− results in an increase of the solution pH (from 7.05 to 10.44) that inhibits the reaction as mentioned in Fig. 6d. Furthermore, the presence of SO4 2− could suppress the formation of ·SO4 − (Long et al., 2021). Apart from anions, HA was used as the NOM representative to investigate the impact on pollutant degradation. As shown in Fig. 6h, the corresponding BPA degradation efficiencies were 88.08 %, 81.45 %, and 78.63 % after the addition of different concentrations (10 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 30 mg/L) of HA, respectively. This is probably due to the fact that unsaturated functional groups in HA could compete with BPA to capture ROS (Maoz and Chefetz, 2010). It could also be that the phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of HA are adsorbed on the active catalytic sites to hinder the reaction (Amiri et al., 2022). Finally, typical water sources with different water quality (Table S3) were chosen to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the 7CNB/PS system (Fig. 6i). As expected, the removal of BPA gradually decreased with increasing water complexity which is attributed to the competition for ROS by small organic molecules. In conclusion, the above results adequately confirm the promising performance of the established visible-light-driven 7CNB/PS system in complex aqueous matrices.

The environmental toxicity of the degradation intermediates was monitored by assessing their effect on the growth of E. coli and B. subtilis. In Fig. 7a, after 12 h of continuous observation, the growth curve (OD600) indicated that the untreated BPA solution significantly inhibited the growth of E. coli. Remarkably, the reaction filtrate after photodegradation promoted the cell proliferation of E. coli. The probable reason is the small intermediate molecules produced during photodegradation which act as a carbon source for E. coli growth (Alavi and Moradi, 2022). However, both the BPA solution and the reaction filtrate had minimal effect on the proliferation of B. subtilis, which could be attributed to the thicker peptidoglycan cell wall structure of the Gram-positive bacteria as shown in Fig. 7b. These findings correspond well with colony densities as presented in Fig. 7c. In addition, the effect of the reaction filtrate on indicators such as intracellular ROS levels, cell cycle, and nonviable cells should be further investigated. The above results show that the environmental toxicity of BPA molecules was significantly alleviated by photodegradation, further demonstrating the eco-friendliness of the 7CNB/PS system.

To explore the main ROS in the photodegradation of the 7CNB/PS system, ROS quenching experiments were performed (experimental setup in Text S2), and the results are shown in Fig. 8a. The BPA degradation efficiency decreased from 100 % to 13.59 %, 35.07 % and 92.85 % after the addition of BQ, EDTA-2Na and MeOH, respectively. ROS quenching experiments indicated that O2 −, h+, SO4 − and OH were involved in the degradation of BPA, especially the contribution of O2 − was the most obvious, followed by h+. In the presence of TBA, the 7CNB/PS system completely removed BPA within 20 min, but the reaction rate constant decreased by a factor of 1.4 compared to that of the blank group (from 0.3646 min−1 to 0.26 min−1, Fig. S5). O2 − and h+ were crucial to influence the photocatalytic activity, while SO4 − and OH also played a minor role. Subsequently, the existence of the presumed ROS was confirmed using EPR techniques. As seen in Fig. 8b, three distinct signals (1:1:1) were observed in the dark and the peak intensity decreased with visible-light continuation, which belonged to the TEMPO-h+ adduct (Qi et al., 2022). Moreover, SO4 −, OH and O2 − were captured using DMPO. No significant signal appeared under dark conditions. However, the appearance of DMPO-SO4 − and DMPO-OH signals after illumination indicated the presence of SO4 − and OH in the 7CNB/PS system as presented in Fig. 8c–d (Li et al., 2019). In addition, the characteristic peak of DMPO-O2 − was also observed. The above EPR spectra show the formation of ROS during BPA photodegradation, which is highly consistent with the results of quenching experiments.

To further investigate the degradation pathway of BPA in the 7CNB/PS system, the intermediates were analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC/MS). As shown in Fig. 9 , there are mainly two degradation pathways depending on the position of bond breakage. Firstly, BPA (m/z = 227) was attacked by ROS in the bridge cleavage process leading to hydroxylation or oxidation to form 3,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid (P1, m/z = 155) (Oh et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the aromatic ring of BPA was further damaged by oxidation after hydroxylation leading to ring opening, resulting in the formation of (2Z)-3-formyl-4-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-4-methylpent-2-enoic acid (P2, m/z = 233) (Si et al., 2021). Furthermore, the aromatic ring continues to be attacked by ROS, leading to the ring opening of P1, which was further oxidized to (2E,4Z)-3-formylhexa-2,4-dienedioic acid (P3, m/z = 173) (Gao et al., 2017). Whereas the already ring-opened P2 could be further oxidized and form 4-(2,3-dimethylbut-3-en-2-yl)phenol (P4, m/z = 175) by decarboxylation (Wan et al., 2022). Finally, the above intermediates were oxidized to small molecule acids and alcohols such as hydroxyacetic acid (P5, m/z = 77) and hexa-1,5-dien-3-ol (P6, m/z = 96) which subsequently mineralized into H2O and CO2.

According to the above discussion, the photocatalytic mechanism of BPA degradation by 7CNB/PS system is shown in Fig. 10 . Under visible light irradiation, the electrons are theoretically not photoexcited for the pristine BOC due to the wide band gap (∼3.34 eV). However, the introduction of N and CDs to replace Cl with N or C or adsorb on Bi changes the photoelectric properties of BiOCl. The band gap of 7CNB is significantly narrower (∼2.37 eV) and the visible light absorption is enhanced compared to that of BOC. Therefore, the electrons of 7CNB would be excited under visible light irradiation (Eq. (4)). Meanwhile, due to the effective electron transport properties of CDs, photogenerated electrons could further migrate to the CDs surface, thus facilitating the charge separation. First, the photoinduced electrons attached to CDs react with PS leading to the OO bond cleavage thereby generating SO4 − (Eq. (5)) to further form OH (Eq. (6)). Subsequently, O2 − are readily produced due to the fact that the reduction potential of the oxygen (E0 O2/O2− = −0.046 eV, vs. NHE) is much more positive than the energy level of the 7CNB CB (Eq. (7)) (Yang et al., 2019). These O2 − could also yield OH (Eq. (8)). Thus, the combined effect form SO4 −, O2 − and OH which oxidizes the BPA molecule (Eq. (10)). Subsequently, when electrons are transferred to the CDs, the holes remain on the VB of 7CNB (Eq. (4)). Although these holes are not favorable for the formation of OH by direct oxidation of water (E0
OH/H2O = 2.68 eV, vs. NHE) (Li et al., 2018a), they could react with micropollutants (Eq. (10)). Similarly, the holes of 7CNB VB could oxidize OH− to produce OH due to the more positive position of EVB than E0
OH/OH- (Eq. (9)). In conclusion, the intermediate energy level formed by N doping effectively shortens the electron migration distance to fully exploit the photoelectric conversion properties of CDs, while the presence of CDs promotes the migration of photogenerated electrons to inhibit the in-situ recombination of carriers. Thus, the photocatalytic performance of BOC is enhanced by the synergistic effect of CDs and N. The possible specific electron transfer processes during the reaction are concluded as follow:

In this study, CDs-N-BiOCl photocatalyst was synthesized by one-step calcination for the photodegradation of BPA. The characterization results showed that the ordered ultrathin nanosheet structure of CNB facilitated the reflection of light to improve the visible-light-harvesting efficiency, while the appearance of COBi, OBiN, and BiON bonds in the BiOCl lattice indicated the substitution of C or N for Cl atoms to tune the built-in electric field. Subsequently, the visible absorption range of CNB and the migration of photogenerated carriers were significantly enhanced under the synergistic influence of CDs loading and N doping as analyzed by UV–vis and PL spectra. The presence of N doping in CDs-BiOCl (CB) composite could effectively facilitate the overall photoelectric conversion efficiency. Conversely, the loading of CDs not only extended the visible light absorption but also significantly inhibited the in-situ recombination of charges in N-BiOCl (NB) photocatalyst. Thus, the established visible-light-driven 7CNB/PS system exhibited the fastest reaction rate (0.3646 min−1) and the highest mineralization (66.4 %) compared to that of N-BiOCl and CDs-BiOCl. Meanwhile, the 7CNB/PS system showed favorable adaptability in a wide pH range, durability after multiple cycles, non-selective oxidation of various contaminants, and resistance to complex water matrices, indicating the potential of the system for practical applications. In addition, the bacterial growth curves suggested that the degraded intermediates facilitated the growth of E. coli. Finally, the electron transfer mechanism of the 7CNB photocatalyst-activated PS for BPA degradation was well explained by combining the energy band structure and EPR results. In summary, the efficient 7CNB/PS system provides a potential value for degrading micro-pollutants in natural water bodies under visible-light irradiation to protect the ecological environment.
